content: This systematic review synthesizes evidence from 23 sources to evaluate the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting (IF) in adults with metabolic syndrome. The review characterizes four principal IF protocols—time-restricted eating, alternate-day fasting, the 5:2 method, and fasting-mimicking diets—and examines their effects on cardiometabolic endpoints including fasting blood glucose, HbA1c, lipid profiles, body weight, BMI, and blood pressure. A PRISMA-compliant meta-analysis of 11 studies pooled data on weight, BMI, cholesterol, blood pressure, and glucose outcomes [7], while a randomized clinical trial of 57 adults with type 2 diabetes demonstrated comparable reductions in HbA1c and body weight across two distinct IF strategies [8]. Mechanistic evidence indicates that IF decreases inflammatory markers including IL-2, IL-6, and RCP [17] and can improve glucose metabolism independently of weight reduction through time-dependent, compartmentalized immune modulation [18]. A meta-analysis incorporating 1,331,468 participants established the elevated cardiovascular mortality risk context within which IF safety must be assessed [14]. Significant evidence gaps persist, including the absence of large-scale, long-duration randomized trials with hard cardiovascular endpoints, limited age-stratified analyses, and incomplete characterization of adverse effects in medically complex populations. The certainty of evidence remains moderate to low for most endpoints due to methodological heterogeneity, short trial durations, and non-standardized IF protocols across studies.
